postData.contentHtml:
Imagine a creature that has quietly thrived beneath the waves for centuries, a living testament to time itself. Such a marvel has recently been unveiled in the mysterious depths of New Zealand's Fiordland. Researchers have discovered an "absolutely huge" black coral, estimated to be between 300 and 400 years old, making it one of the largest ever recorded in Aotearoa New Zealand.

This ancient giant measures an impressive 4 meters (13 feet) tall and a staggering 4.5 meters (15 feet) wide. Professor James Bell, a marine biologist with 25 years of experience from Te Herenga Waka – Victoria University of Wellington, described the find as unprecedented in his career. "It's by far the largest black coral I've seen in my 25 years as a marine biologist," he stated. Richard Kinsey, a senior biodiversity ranger with the Department of Conservation, echoed this sentiment, remarking on the coral "looming out of the darkness" and calling it easily the largest he'd seen in nearly two decades in Fiordland.

The discovery is particularly significant given the incredibly slow growth rate of black corals. Larger, older colonies like this one play a vital role in the species' survival by providing valuable breeding stock. Recognizing this, researchers are actively mapping coral distributions throughout the fiords to guide future conservation efforts. Professor Bell urged the public to report any other large black coral sightings, emphasizing the importance of knowing where these crucial habitats exist.

Despite its intriguing name, living black coral is not black on the outside; its vibrant white polyps mask an internal black skeleton. This remarkable species is protected under the Wildlife Act, making it illegal to intentionally collect or damage it. The overwhelmingly positive social sentiment surrounding this discovery underscores a global appreciation for such natural wonders and the critical need for their protection. This find serves as a powerful reminder of the hidden treasures that still await discovery in our oceans and the urgent call to preserve these delicate deep-sea ecosystems for generations to come.
postData.title: Ancient Giant Unveiled: 400-Year-Old Black Coral Discovered in New Zealand's Deep
postData.summary: A colossal 14-foot black coral, estimated to be up to 400 years old, has been found thriving in the mysterious deep waters of Fiordland, New Zealand, astonishing scientists and highlighting crucial conservation needs.

sources.title: “Absolutely huge” 400-year-old black coral stuns scientists in New Zealand - ScienceDaily
A 14-Foot Black Coral Hid in New Zealand's Deep for 400 Years - MSN
